notes: California has released a comprehensive, non-mandatory guidance document for local educational agencies (LEAs). The guidance is explicitly "human-centered" and provides detailed recommendations and guiding questions on key issues including academic integrity, data privacy, and educator professional development. It encourages thoughtful, safe, and effective integration of AI into schools while emphasizing that the guidance is informative rather than prescriptive.

notes: North Carolina's Department of Public Instruction (NCDPI) released a comprehensive guidebook with recommendations for generative AI implementation in January 2024. The guidance is strong across all categories, providing an implementation roadmap for districts, detailed frameworks for responsible use (e.g., 'EVERY framework'), and specific recommendations for adapting assessments (e.g., 'AI Resistant, AI Assisted, AI Partnered Assignments'). The policy strongly emphasizes building educator capacity through differentiated professional learning, coaching, and microcredentialing. It also includes robust guidance on student data privacy, vendor management, and academic integrity. The guidebook is a living document with a version history showing regular updates, indicating an operationalized and recurring system.

notes: This document is a model policy developed by the Ohio Department of Education and Workforce to guide local districts. A state law requires all public, community, and STEM schools to adopt a formal policy on AI use by July 1, 2026. The model policy provides comprehensive and strong guidance across all categories, including acceptable use, academic integrity, data privacy and security, and the need for professional learning to build AI literacy for educators.

notes: The Oregon Department of Education (ODE) has published a comprehensive webpage with resources and guidance for K-12 schools. The state provides two key guidance documents: "Generative Artificial Intelligence in K-12 Classrooms" and "Developing Policy and Protocols for the Use of Generative AI in K-12 Classrooms." The state also curates an extensive list of resources for teacher professional development, AI literacy for students, and links to sample policies from local districts. The guidance focuses on supporting districts in developing their own policies for the safe, ethical, and effective use of AI.

notes: The document is a comprehensive framework from the Utah State Board of Education. It provides guiding principles, examples of responsible and prohibited uses, and special considerations for privacy and security. The document is dated March 2026, suggesting it may be a forward-looking plan or a draft, but it is presented as a complete guidance document. It emphasizes a balanced approach, avoiding an outright ban while establishing clear guardrails for safe and ethical implementation.

evidence_spans.quote: This document guides Utah students, staff, and school communities on the appropriate and responsible use of artificial intelligence (AI), particularly generative AI tools, in classroom instruction, school management, and system-wide operations where permitted by local policy.

Students and staff should not copy from any source, including generative AI, without prior approval and adequate documentation. Students should not submit AI-generated work as their original work. Staff and students will be taught how to properly cite or acknowledge the use of AI where applicable.

Staff and students are prohibited from entering confidential or personally identifiable information into unauthorized AI tools, such as those without approved data privacy agreements. This extends to contracted vendors and other third parties.

We educate our staff and students about AI. Promoting AI literacy among students and staff is central to addressing the risks of AI use and teaches critical skills for students' futures. Students and staff will be given support to develop their AI literacy, which includes how to use AI, when to use it,...
Artificial Intelligence Framework for Utah P-12 Education: Guidance on the Use of AI in Our Schools

Conversely, an outright ban on AI in schools impedes the development of essential future workforce skills. Instead, a balanced and informed approach is preferred, encompassing both maximizing learning and attending to security measures.

verification_notes: Human review confirmed the Utah AI Framework PDF supports the balanced, guardrail-heavy coding profile.

notes: Washington's Office of Superintendent of Public Instruction (OSPI) released formal guidance in January 2024. It provides specific guidance on ethical applications, including academic integrity and data privacy, and includes a list of topics for educator professional learning.
